div.h2: Kernel vs. Userspace Virtual Address Space (x86-64)

p.em: Every process sees this full map; hardware enforces the privilege boundary

div.p: SMAP (Supervisor Mode Access Prevention) CPU CR4.SMAP bit — ring-0 code faults if it directly touches any address below TASK_SIZE_MAX without STAC/CLAC bracketing. copy_from_user / copy_to_user issue STAC before the copy and CLAC after.
Each process sees identical virtual addresses (e.g., stack at 0x7fff…) but CPU page tables map them to different physical pages. Processes are isolated from each other.

text.tspan: CPU: saves rsp, switches privilege; TLB NOT flushed (PCID)
Kernel sees both halves; SMAP blocks direct user-ptr deref

ol.li: STAC — CPU allows ring-0 to touch user pages

div.p: Never use memcpy / plain deref on a __user pointer.
Kernel mapped into every process The purple kernel region appears in every process's page table, but CR0.WP + page-table USER bit prevents ring-3 access. One physical copy of kernel text serves all.

text.tspan: access_ok() rejects any userspace ptr ≥ this value | SMAP hardware enforcement line
first 4 KB not mapped → NULL deref → SIGSEGV
~2–4 KB | symbol table, refcnt, init/exit ptrs
per-CPU stacks + interrupt stacks 8 KB each
current task context during syscall / insmod
(may be swapped — handled by copy_from_user pagefault)
process B cannot see process A's memory

g.text: all kernel↔user data transfer goes through
purple region present in all page tables
